critique.summary: The article explores the use of visually-hidden classes in web design, particularly their relevance and effectiveness in 2026. It discusses the history, purpose, and various implementations of these classes while highlighting the ongoing debate about their appropriateness for accessibility.
mainPoints: The visually-hidden class is used to hide elements visually while making them accessible to screen readers.
There are many variations of the visually-hidden class, and its effectiveness is debated in the context of modern web design.
critique.truthfulness: The article provides a generally accurate overview of the visually-hidden class and its purpose in web accessibility. It correctly identifies the challenges and limitations of using such classes, particularly in relation to screen reader compatibility. However, the article lacks specific examples of current best practices and may benefit from more concrete guidelines for implementation.
considerations: The article does not address the impact of using visually-hidden classes on user experience beyond accessibility.
It does not mention newer techniques or standards that may have emerged since the original visually-hidden class was developed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the challenges and frustrations of designing for accessibility, particularly with screen readers and CSS solutions. Participants express their experiences and opinions on the current state of accessibility standards.

comments.author: jfengel
comments.text: I wish I understood screen readers. Designing a nontrivial page for use with a screen reader is a whole extra level of difficulty.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a genuine concern for accessibility and acknowledges the complexities involved in designing for screen readers.

comments.author: akersten
comments.text: This is very frustrating as someone who has seen every iteration of this in practice. It's wild to me that we don't simply have a `display: accessibility-tree-only` CSS prop...
comments.analysis: The comment articulates frustration with current standards while proposing a specific solution, demonstrating both emotional investment and technical insight.

comments.author: PeterStuer
comments.text: The level of absurdity some sites go to to ruin accesibility in the name of some holy crusade against "ai-bots" is beyond the pale.
comments.analysis: While the comment expresses a valid concern about accessibility issues, its tone is more confrontational and less constructive.

comments.author: amelius
comments.text: The headlines are getting weirder every day.
comments.analysis: This comment lacks relevance to the main discussion and does not contribute meaningfully to the topic at hand.

comments.author: elophanto_agent
comments.text: visually-hidden is the CSS equivalent of "I'm not touching you" — technically accessible, technically invisible...
comments.analysis: This comment cleverly compares CSS practices to a common phrase, but it may come off as dismissive rather than constructive.
